source_paper_title: Is There a Golden Parachute in Sannikov’s Principal–Agent Problem?
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Possama__2025, title={Is There a Golden Parachute in Sannikov’s Principal–Agent Problem?}, volume={50}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2022.0305}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2022.0305}, number={2}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Possamaï, Dylan and Touzi, Nizar}, year={2025}, month=may, pages={1173–1203} }

However, in the case \(\delta > 1\), it is unclear in general whether \(y_{gp}\) is finite or not, and we do not have sufficient conditions under which it is (though we present conditions under which \(y_{gp} = 0\) in Section 3.2).
title: Determine whether Golden Parachutes arise with an impatient agent setting
background_latex:
Consider Sannikov’s continuous-time principal–agent model with moral hazard over an endogenous retirement time.

1) Output and effort. The output process \(X\) is observed and evolves under the agent’s effort process \(\alpha=(\alpha_t)_{t\ge0}\) as
\[dX_t=\alpha_t\,dt+\sigma\,dW_t,\qquad \sigma>0,\]
where \(W\) is a Brownian motion under the measure induced by \(\alpha\). The effort \(\alpha_t\) takes values in a compact set \(A\subset[0,\infty)\) containing \(0\). The agent incurs instantaneous cost \(h(\alpha_t)\), where \(h:[0,\infty)\to[0,\infty)\) is increasing, strictly convex, \(C^1\), and \(h(0)=0\). Denote \(\beta:=h'(0)\).

2) Contract. A contract \(C=(\tau,\pi,\xi)\) consists of a stopping time \(\tau\) (retirement time), a nonnegative predictable payment rate \(\pi_t\) paid up to \(\tau\), and a nonnegative lump-sum payment \(\xi\) at time \(\tau\).

3) Preferences and discounting. The agent has discount rate \(r>0\) and flow utility \(u(\pi_t)\) from consumption, where \(u:[0,\infty)\to[0,\infty)\) is increasing, strictly concave, \(C^2\) on \((0,\infty)\), with \(u(0)=0\) and Inada tail condition \(\lim_{x\to\infty}u'(x)=0\). The principal is risk-neutral with discount rate \(\rho>0\). Let \(\delta:=r/\rho\).

4) Agent’s value for a contract \(C\). For a given effort \(\alpha\), the agent’s expected utility is
\[J_A(C,\alpha)=\mathbb E\Big[e^{-r\tau}u(\xi)+\int_0^{\tau} r e^{-rs}\big(u(\pi_s)-h(\alpha_s)\big)\,ds\Big].\]
The agent chooses \(\alpha\) to maximize \(J_A(C,\alpha)\), and the contract must satisfy a participation constraint \(V_A(C)\ge u(R)\) for some reservation level \(R\ge0\).

5) Principal’s value. Anticipating the agent’s optimal response, the principal chooses \(C\) to maximize her discounted expected payoff.

6) “Golden Parachute” (as defined in the paper). A Golden Parachute exists if there is an optimal contract \((\tau^*,\pi^*,\xi^*)\) such that \(\tau^*>0\) and \(\mathbb P(\xi^*>0)>0\), i.e., the agent is retired at a strictly positive time and receives a positive post-retirement payment (lump-sum and/or equivalent lifetime stream).

7) Regime of interest. The paper distinguishes regimes by \(\delta\). The open issue below concerns the case \(\delta>1\), i.e., the agent is strictly more impatient than the principal.
problem_definition_latex:
In the above model, focus on the regime \(\delta=r/\rho>1\) (agent strictly more impatient than the principal). Let \(V(y)\) denote the principal’s value when the agent’s continuation utility is held at level \(y\ge0\), and let \(F\) denote the (face-lifted) retirement reward function against which retirement is compared.

Determine whether the associated optimal stopping threshold \(y_{\mathrm{gp}}\in[0,\infty]\) at which the principal retires the agent is finite or infinite, i.e. decide whether the stopping region \(\{y: V(y)=F(y)\}\) takes the form \(\{0\}\cup[y_{\mathrm{gp}},\infty)\) with \(y_{\mathrm{gp}}<\infty\) (existence of a Golden Parachute), or whether in general \(y_{\mathrm{gp}}=\infty\) (no Golden Parachute), in the regime \(\delta>1\).

Consider Sannikov’s continuous-time principal–agent model with moral hazard over an endogenous retirement time.

1) Output and effort. The output process \(X\) is observed and evolves under the agent’s effort process \(\alpha=(\alpha_t)_{t\ge0}\) as
\[dX_t=\alpha_t\,dt+\sigma\,dW_t,\qquad \sigma>0,\]
where \(W\) is a Brownian motion under the measure induced by \(\alpha\). The effort \(\alpha_t\) takes values in a compact set \(A\subset[0,\infty)\) containing \(0\). The agent incurs instantaneous cost \(h(\alpha_t)\), where \(h:[0,\infty)\to[0,\infty)\) is increasing, strictly convex, \(C^1\), and \(h(0)=0\). Denote \(\beta:=h'(0)\).

2) Contract. A contract \(C=(\tau,\pi,\xi)\) consists of a stopping time \(\tau\) (retirement time), a nonnegative predictable payment rate \(\pi_t\) paid up to \(\tau\), and a nonnegative lump-sum payment \(\xi\) at time \(\tau\).

The agent has discount rate \(r>0\) and flow utility \(u(\pi_t)\) from consumption, where \(u:[0,\infty)\to[0,\infty)\) is increasing, strictly concave, \(C^2\) on \((0,\infty)\), with \(u(0)=0\) and Inada tail condition \(\lim_{x\to\infty}u'(x)=0\). The principal is risk-neutral with discount rate \(\rho>0\). Let \(\delta:=r/\rho\).

4) Agent’s value for a contract \(C\). For a given effort \(\alpha\), the agent’s expected utility is
\[J_A(C,\alpha)=\mathbb E\Big[e^{-r\tau}u(\xi)+\int_0^{\tau} r e^{-rs}\big(u(\pi_s)-h(\alpha_s)\big)\,ds\Big].\]
The agent chooses \(\alpha\) to maximize \(J_A(C,\alpha)\), and the contract must satisfy a participation constraint \(V_A(C)\ge u(R)\) for some reservation level \(R\ge0\).

A Golden Parachute exists if there is an optimal contract \((\tau^*,\pi^*,\xi^*)\) such that \(\tau^*>0\) and \(\mathbb P(\xi^*>0)>0\), i.e., the agent is retired at a strictly positive time and receives a positive post-retirement payment (lump-sum and/or equivalent lifetime stream).

7) Regime of interest. The paper distinguishes regimes by \(\delta\). The open issue below concerns the case \(\delta>1\), i.e., the agent is strictly more impatient than the principal.

In the above model, focus on the regime \(\delta=r/\rho>1\) (agent strictly more impatient than the principal). Let \(V(y)\) denote the principal’s value when the agent’s continuation utility is held at level \(y\ge0\), and let \(F\) denote the (face-lifted) retirement reward function against which retirement is compared.

Determine whether the associated optimal stopping threshold \(y_{\mathrm{gp}}\in[0,\infty]\) at which the principal retires the agent is finite or infinite, i.e. decide whether the stopping region \(\{y: V(y)=F(y)\}\) takes the form \(\{0\}\cup[y_{\mathrm{gp}},\infty)\) with \(y_{\mathrm{gp}}<\infty\) (existence of a Golden Parachute), or whether in general \(y_{\mathrm{gp}}=\infty\) (no Golden Parachute), in the regime \(\delta>1\).
location_in_paper: Page 9, Remark 3.5(iii) (end of Section 3.1, before Section 3.2).
open_problem_quote: However, in the case \(\delta > 1\), it is unclear in general whether \(y_{gp}\) is finite or not, and we do not have sufficient conditions under which it is (though we present conditions under which \(y_{gp} = 0\) in Section 3.2).
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The question reduces to whether the free boundary/optimal stopping threshold is finite for given parametric specifications, which can be probed numerically by solving the associated HJB/ODE and checking contact with the obstacle. Instance search over utility/cost parameterizations is feasible, but the space is infinite-dimensional unless restricted to parametric families.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: One can parameterize utility and cost functions (e.g., power utility, polynomial/quadratic effort costs) and compute the resulting value function numerically (solve the HJB variational inequality) to estimate whether the stopping boundary is finite, yielding an automatic fitness signal (e.g., detected contact point or gap to the obstacle). An evolutionary loop can search over parameters to find examples supporting finiteness/infiniteness and suggest conjectured sufficient conditions.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
In Possamaï–Touzi’s reformulation of Sannikov’s model with endogenous retirement, the principal’s second-best value as a function of the agent’s continuation utility \(y\) solves an infinite-horizon 1D obstacle problem \(\min\{V(y)-F(y),\;LV(y)\}=0\), where the obstacle \(F\) is a face-lifted retirement reward capturing the principal’s ability (when discount rates differ) to postpone retirement while keeping the agent indifferent via a deterministic post-retirement consumption plan. The open issue is precisely whether, in the impatient-agent regime \(\delta=r/\rho>1\), the stopping set \(\{V=F\}\) has a finite upper boundary \([y_{\mathrm{gp}},\infty)\) (Golden Parachute) or whether the boundary is at infinity (no Golden Parachute). The source paper proves structural results (e.g., \(\{V=F\}=\{0\}\cup[y_{\mathrm{gp}},\infty)\) under concavity assumptions) but explicitly leaves open whether \(y_{\mathrm{gp}}<\infty\) can be guaranteed for \(\delta>1\); their numerics suggest that \(V\) may stay strictly above \(F\) for all \(y>0\) in some \(\delta>1\) parameterizations.

Forward citations largely develop tools and adjacent models rather than resolving this free-boundary question. Lin–Ren–Touzi–Yang’s “Random horizon principal-agent problems” provides a general reduction of random-horizon principal–agent Stackelberg games to control/optimal stopping problems in the continuation-utility state, thereby supplying a rigorous pathway to derive the relevant HJB/obstacle equation in broad settings, but it does not analyze the \(\delta>1\) boundary behavior. “Golden parachutes under the threat of accidents” extends the face-lifting/termination-reward analysis to models with jump accidents and derives explicit variational inequalities for the face-lifted reward under \(\delta>1\), yet it still does not characterize the stopping region \(\{v^{SB}=\bar F\}\) (hence does not decide finiteness of a retirement threshold). Several works in PPP or ambiguity extensions (e.g., “Optimal stopping contract for public private partnerships under moral hazard” and “Moral Hazard, Dynamic Incentives, and Ambiguous Perceptions”) similarly reduce to 1D variational inequalities/HJBI equations and often exhibit finite stopping thresholds in numerics or assume such a structure for verification, but they do not prove the finiteness/infinity dichotomy in the original Sannikov/Possamaï–Touzi \(\delta>1\) regime.

Overall, based on the provided forward-citation set, there is no paper that proves \(y_{\mathrm{gp}}<\infty\) or \(y_{\mathrm{gp}}=\infty\) in general for \(\delta>1\) in the Brownian drift-control model with face-lifted obstacle \(F\). The problem therefore remains open. Promising directions suggested by the literature include: (i) exploiting the dual formulation in terms of \(p=V'(y)\) (as in the source paper’s dual ODE heuristics for \(\delta>1\)) to obtain sharp asymptotics of \(V-F\) as \(y\to\infty\); (ii) establishing comparison/monotonicity properties that would force eventual contact with the obstacle or preclude it; and (iii) combining the general random-horizon reduction and viscosity/regularity techniques with parameter-restricted explicit examples to delineate regions of \(\delta>1\) where contact must (or cannot) occur.

sources.title: Random horizon principal-agent problems
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper proves a general reduction theorem for random-horizon principal–agent problems with continuous payments and a terminal lump-sum: under suitable integrability and existence of Hamiltonian maximizers, the Stackelberg game (European case: principal chooses termination \,\(\tau^P\) and payments \,\((\pi,\xi)\)) is equivalent to a stochastic control problem over the agent’s continuation utility process \,\(Y\) represented by 
\[Y_t=Y_0+\int_0^t \Big(Z_s\cdot dX_s+\tfrac12\mathrm{Tr}(\Gamma_s d\langle X\rangle_s)-H_s(Y_s,Z_s,\Gamma_s)\,ds-U_A(\pi_s)\,ds\Big),\]
with terminal transfer \,\(\xi=U_A^{-1}(Y_{\tau^P})\). In the American case (agent can quit), the principal’s problem reduces similarly with endogenous termination time \,\(h_0:=\inf\{t\ge0: Y_t\le U_A(\rho)\}\). (Theorem 4.2, plus density Proposition 4.4 via random-horizon 2BSDEs.)
sources.connection: The open problem asks, in Sannikov’s model with endogenous retirement and discount-rate ratio \(\delta=r/\rho>1\), whether the optimal stopping threshold \(y_{\mathrm{gp}}\) for principal-initiated retirement is finite (golden parachute) or infinite. This paper does not analyze that specific obstacle/free-boundary problem or the \(\delta>1\) regime. Instead it provides a general framework reducing random-horizon principal–agent problems (including Sannikov’s) to stochastic control/optimal stopping problems in the agent’s continuation utility state \(Y\), and derives the corresponding HJB/obstacle equations in examples (Section 5.1). It therefore supplies tools and a reduction that can be used to study the threshold \(y_{\mathrm{gp}}\), but it does not determine whether \(y_{\mathrm{gp}}<\infty\) or \(=\infty\) when \(\delta>1\).

sources.title: Golden parachutes under the threat of accidents
sources.theorem_or_result:
In a Sannikov-type principal–agent model with accidents (compound Poisson losses) and possibly different discount rates 
\(\delta=r/\rho\), the paper derives an explicit characterization of the face-lifted retirement/termination reward \(\bar F\) via the variational inequality
\[\min\{\bar F(y)-F(y),\; F^\star(\delta \bar F'(y)) - \delta y\bar F'(y) + \bar F(y) + m\}=0,\ y>0,\ \bar F(0)=0.\]
For \(\delta>1\):
- If \(m\le -F^\star(\delta F'(0))\), then \(\bar F=(w^\star)^\star\) and \(\bar F>F\) on \((0,\infty)\).
- If \(m> -F^\star(\delta F'(0))\), then \(\bar F(y)=F(y)\) on \([0,\bar y]\) where \(\bar y:=\inf\{y\ge0: F^\star(\delta F'(y)) -\delta yF'(y)+F(y)+m\le0\}\), and \(\bar F=(w^\star)^\star\) on \((\bar y,\infty)\).
For the second-best problem, the principal’s value \(v^{SB}\) is characterized as the unique continuous viscosity solution of an integro-differential obstacle problem
\[\min\{v^{SB}(y)-\bar F(y),\; F^\star(\delta (v^{SB})'(y)) -\delta y (v^{SB})'(y) + v^{SB}(y) - \mathcal J^{SB}(y,(v^{SB})'(y),(v^{SB})''(y),v^{SB})\}=0,\ y>0,\ v^{SB}(0)=0,\]
but the paper does not characterize the stopping set \(\{y: v^{SB}(y)=\bar F(y)\}\) (hence no explicit finiteness/infinity of a retirement threshold is proved).
sources.connection: The open problem asks, in the original Brownian Sannikov/Possama

sources.title: Optimal stopping contract for public private partnerships under moral hazard
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper formulates and (numerically) solves a continuous-time principal–agent problem with moral hazard and endogenous stopping (PPP contract termination) by reducing it to a 1D HJB variational inequality for the principal’s value function \(v(x)\) where \(x\) is the agent’s continuation utility. The value function satisfies
\[\min\Big(\delta v(x)-\sup_{(r,a)\in\mathbb R_+^2}\{\mathcal L^{a,r}v(x)+\varphi(a)-r\},\ v(x)+U^{-1}(x)\Big)=0,\quad x>0,\]
with boundary \(v(0)=0\) and stopping region \(\{x: v(x)\le -U^{-1}(x)\}\). Under smoothness/verification assumptions, the optimal stopping time is the first hitting time of the boundary of the continuation region \((0,\hat b)\), i.e. \(\tau^*=\inf\{t\ge0: J^C_t\in\{0,\hat b\}\}\), and the optimal rent is \(r^*(x)=(U')^{-1}(-1/v'(x))\mathbf 1_{v'(x)<0}\).
sources.connection: The open problem asks, in Sannikov’s model with endogenous retirement, whether in the impatient-agent regime \(\delta=r/\rho>1\) the optimal retirement threshold \(y_{\mathrm{gp}}\) is finite (Golden Parachute) or infinite. This paper cites Possamaï–Touzi on golden parachutes and studies a related principal–agent-with-stopping model, deriving an HJB variational inequality and exhibiting a finite stopping threshold \(\hat b\) in numerical examples. However: (i) its model differs materially (PPP setting, bounded concave drift impact \(\varphi\), different discount notation \(\delta,\lambda\), and terminal payoff \(-U^{-1}(x)\) rather than the face-lifted retirement reward \(F\) in the golden-parachute paper); (ii) it does not analyze the regime corresponding to \(r/\rho>1\) in the original question nor prove finiteness/infiniteness of the stopping boundary in general; (iii) results are primarily verification + numerics for chosen parameters. Thus it provides partial, indirect evidence/tools but not a resolution of \(y_{\mathrm{gp}}\) in the stated model/regime.

sources.title: Moral Hazard, Dynamic Incentives, and Ambiguous Perceptions
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper formulates a Sannikov-style continuous-time moral hazard model with drift ambiguity and endogenous retirement/termination (finite random horizon), derives a recursive HJBI equation for the principal’s value as a function of the agent’s continuation value, and (Theorem 1) proves a verification result: if the HJBI admits a unique smooth solution and the stopping region is of the form \(S=\{0\}\cup[W_{gp},\infty)\) with finite \(W_{gp}<\infty\), then the associated feedback controls \((c(W),a(W),Y(W))\) implement an optimal contract with retirement time \(\tau=\inf\{t:W_t\in S\}\).
sources.connection: The open problem asks, in the standard Sannikov model with different discount rates and endogenous retirement, whether the golden-parachute stopping threshold \(y_{gp}\) is finite or infinite in the impatient-agent regime \(\delta=r/\rho>1\). This citing paper does not analyze that regime: it assumes a common discount rate \(r\) for principal and agent and adds drift ambiguity. It explicitly assumes (rather than proves) that the stopping region has a finite threshold \(W_{gp}<\infty\) to state its verification theorem, and it notes that ambiguity has an ambiguous effect on duration/retirement. Thus it does not resolve whether \(y_{gp}\) is finite/infinite for \(\delta>1\), but it provides related methodology (HJBI/verification with stopping) and discusses golden-parachute clauses in an extended model.

sources.title: Contracting with discretionary bonuses
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper formulates a finite-horizon principal–agent model with up to \(N\) discretionary lump-sum bonuses and proves (Theorem 2) that the principal’s value \(V_{p,n}(t,y)\) is the unique continuous viscosity solution of the recursive parabolic HJB variational inequalities
\[
\min\{-\partial_t v_{p,n}+Gv_{p,n},\ v_{p,n}-Mv_{p,n-1},\ -\partial_{yy}v_{p,n}\}=0,\quad n=1,\dots,N,
\]
with terminal condition \(v_{p,n}(T-,y)=g_n(y)=M^nF(y)\) and boundary \(v_{p,n}(t,0)=0\), where \(M\) is the impulse (bonus) operator \(MV(t,y)=\sup_{0\le \eta\le y}\{V(t,y-\eta)-(1+k)\eta^\gamma\}\). It also gives a feedback characterization of the optimal bonus size \(\eta^*_{n}(t,y)\) on the intervention region \(\{v_{p,n}=Mv_{p,n-1}\}\) via a first-order condition on \(\partial_y V_{p,n-1}\) (equation (20)).
sources.connection: The open problem asks, in Sannikov’s infinite-horizon endogenous-retirement model, whether for \(\delta=r/\rho>1\) the optimal retirement (stopping) threshold \(y_{\mathrm{gp}}\) is finite (golden parachute) or infinite (no golden parachute). This paper does not study endogenous retirement/termination as an optimal stopping boundary against a retirement reward \(F\); instead it fixes a finite horizon \(T\) and models discretionary bonus payments as impulses at stopping times, with a terminal payment at \(T\). Theorem 2 provides a rigorous viscosity-solution characterization for the principal’s value with impulses, and the numerics (Figures 8 and 11) explore the regime \(\delta>1\), showing regions where an initial bonus (“golden hello”) may occur and how intervention regions change with \(N\). However, it does not establish analytically whether an infinite-horizon retirement stopping region \(\{V=F\}\) has a finite threshold \(y_{\mathrm{gp}}\) when \(\delta>1\), nor does it prove existence/nonexistence of golden parachutes in that setting. At best it offers related methods (mixed control/stopping/impulse HJB-VIs) and numerical evidence in a different finite-horizon, multi-bonus model.

sources.title: Contrat optimal pour les partenariats public-privé avec aléa moral: une approche de contrôle stochastique
sources.theorem_or_result:
The thesis develops a continuous-time principal–agent model with moral hazard and endogenous stopping (termination) and derives the principal’s value as the solution of a Hamilton–Jacobi–Bellman variational inequality. In Chapter 3, with agent discount rate \(\lambda\) and principal discount rate \(\delta\), the principal’s value \(v(x)\) (state \(x\)=agent continuation value) satisfies
\[
\min\Big(\delta v(x)-\sup_{(r,a)\in\mathbb R_+^2}\{\mathcal L_{a,r}v(x)+\varphi(a)-r\},\ v(x)+x\Big)=0,\quad x>0,
\]
with boundary \(v(0)=0\), where \(\mathcal L_{a,r}v(x)=\tfrac12(\sigma h'(a)/\varphi'(a))^2\mathbf 1_{a>0}v''(x)+[\lambda x-U(r)+h(a)]v'(x)\). The optimal stopping time is \(\tau^*=\inf\{t\ge0: v(J_t^C)\le -J_t^C\}\) for the optimally controlled continuation value process \(J_t^C\).
sources.connection: The open problem asks, in Sannikov’s model with endogenous retirement and differing discount rates \(\delta=r/\rho>1\), whether the optimal retirement (golden-parachute) threshold \(y_{\mathrm{gp}}\) is finite or infinite. This thesis treats a closely related class of continuous-time moral-hazard contracts with endogenous stopping and allows different discount rates between principal and agent (Chapter 3 uses \(\lambda\) for the agent and \(\delta\) for the principal). It sets up and verifies the HJB variational inequality and characterizes the stopping region via \(v(x)\le -x\). However, the thesis does not (in the provided text) analyze the qualitative dichotomy ‘finite vs infinite stopping threshold’ as a function of impatience ratio, nor does it match the exact Sannikov/Possamaï–Touzi golden-parachute face-lifting formulation \(V=F\) and the threshold \(y_{\mathrm{gp}}\) for \(\delta>1\). Thus it provides methodology and a related stopping characterization, but not a resolution of the specific golden-parachute threshold question.
